title: 2  Introduction to the UKNEHS
text:
Hearing loss is one of the most common chronic health conditions worldwide and represents a major and growing public health challenge. It is estimated that over 430 million people globally live with disabling hearing loss, with projections suggesting this number will exceed 700 million by 2050 as populations age and exposure to environmental risk factors accumulates (World Health Organization, 2021). Beyond its effects on auditory function, hearing loss is increasingly recognised as a condition with wide-ranging consequences for health and wellbeing, including impacts on communication, social participation, mental health and quality of life (Mick, Kawachi and Lin, 2014; Cosh et al., 2018).
In addition to these functional effects, hearing loss has been associated with a range of adverse health outcomes, including cognitive decline, dementia, frailty and increased risk of falls (Lin et al., 2011; Deal et al., 2015; Maharani et al., 2018). These associations have led to growing recognition of hearing loss as a potentially modifiable risk factor for healthy ageing, and a key target for public health intervention (Livingston et al., 2020). Despite this, hearing loss remains under-recognised and undertreated, with substantial delays often occurring between onset of symptoms, diagnosis and intervention.
Robust population-level data are essential to understand the burden of hearing loss, identify inequalities in access to care and inform healthcare planning. However, in the United Kingdom, contemporary nationally representative data on hearing health remain limited. The most widely cited estimates of hearing loss prevalence are still derived from the National Study of Hearing conducted several decades ago (Davis, 1995), and are unlikely to reflect current population demographics, patterns of risk exposure or service provision. More recent studies, including large cohort datasets such as UK Biobank, have provided valuable insights but are not designed to provide nationally representative estimates and often rely on limited or indirect measures of hearing function (Dawes et al., 2014). Internationally, several large population-based studies have demonstrated the value of comprehensive sensory epidemiology. Studies such as the Beaver Dam cohort, the Blue Mountains Eye Study and the Rotterdam Study have provided important insights into the prevalence, determinants and consequences of sensory impairment in ageing populations (Mitchell et al., 1995; Cruickshanks et al., 1998; Hofman et al., 2015). More recently, national surveys such as the Australian Eye and Ear Health Survey have highlighted the feasibility and value of assessing hearing and vision together within a representative population sample using pure tone audiometry testing and detailed phenotyping (Kha et al., 2024).
Despite these advances, comparable contemporary data on hearing health in the UK are lacking. In particular, there is limited evidence describing the distribution and severity of hearing loss based on audiometric assessment within representative populations, the relationship between measured hearing loss and self-reported symptoms, and the extent of unmet need for intervention. These gaps limit the ability to plan services, evaluate policy and target interventions effectively.
The present study reports findings from the hearing component of the UKNEHS pilot. Specifically, we aim to (1) describe the prevalence and severity of hearing loss using audiometric assessment, (2) examine the relationship between measured hearing loss and self-reported hearing difficulties, and (3) explore variation in hearing health across community and care-home populations. By providing contemporary data using portable, scalable assessment methods, this study also informs the feasibility of a future national sensory epidemiology programme.

title: 3  Methods
text:
4 Study design and setting
This study reports findings from the hearing component of the UK National Eye and Hearing Study (UKNEHS) pilot, a cross-sectional population-based study designed to assess the feasibility of conducting a national sensory epidemiology programme in the United Kingdom. Participants were recruited across three settings: a dedicated research site, assessments conducted in participants’ own homes, and care-home environments.


5 Participants
Adults aged 50 years and older were recruited using a combination of community-based approaches and targeted recruitment within care-home settings. Participants were eligible if they were able to undergo hearing assessment and provide informed consent. Individuals with incomplete audiometric data were excluded from the primary hearing analysis.


6 Audiometric assessment
Hearing was assessed using air conduction pure-tone audiometry conducted by trained personnel using calibrated, portable audiometric equipment (including TympaHealth and hearX systems), enabling assessment across both clinical and non-clinical environments. Assessments were conducted in accordance with a standardised audiometric protocol across all settings.Testing was conducted in quiet environments using noise-attenuating headphones, with procedures adapted for non-clinical settings to optimise test reliability while maintaining feasibility. Prior to audiometric testing, otoscopic examination was performed, and obstructing cerumen was removed where necessary to allow accurate assessment. Air conduction thresholds were obtained at standard audiometric frequencies. The primary outcome measure was the pure-tone average (PTA) across 500, 1000, 2000 and 4000 Hz in the better-hearing ear. Hearing loss was defined as a PTA ≥25 dB HL and categorised according to World Health Organization (WHO) severity thresholds. Self-reported measures and hearing aid use Participants provided self-reported information on hearing difficulty using structured questionnaires administered at the time of assessment. Hearing aid use was recorded based on participant self-report..


7 Statistical Methods
All analyses were performed in R 4.5.3 (R Project for Statistical Computing, Vienna, Austria). Participants with incomplete audiometric data were excluded from the primary analysis. Hearing loss was defined using the better-hearing ear pure-tone average (PTA) across 500, 1000, 2000 and 4000 Hz. Hearing loss was defined as a PTA ≥20 dB HL, and severity was classified according to World Health Organization (WHO) hearing loss categories. Participant characteristics were summarised overall and by hearing status. Continuous variables were described using median and IQR, and categorical variables using frequencies and percentages. Comparisons between groups were performed using Kruskal–Wallis rank-sum tests for continuous variables and Pearson’s χ² tests or Fisher’s exact tests for categorical variables, as appropriate. To account for multiple testing in exploratory univariable analyses, p values were adjusted using correction using false-discovery rate. Primary analyses were descriptive and examined the prevalence and severity of hearing loss overall and by recruitment setting. Agreement between audiometric hearing loss and self-reported hearing difficulty was also assessed. Hearing aid use was described according to audiometric hearing status and severity. Analyses involving questionnaire-derived variables were restricted to participants with complete data for those measures. A two-sided p value <0.05 was considered statistically significant, unless a false discovery rate was conducted, in which a q value <0.05 was considered statistically significant.

title: 4  Our findings in relation to hearing impairment
text:
5 Participant characteristics
A total of 560 participants were recruited to the UKNEHS pilot across three settings: 362 at the research site, 84 assessed in their own homes and 114 in care homes. Of these, 423 participants had complete audiometric data and were included in the primary analysis. Participants with incomplete audiometry (n = 137) were excluded. Among participants with valid audiometry, 152 ([48%]) were classified as having hearing loss (better ear pure-tone average ≥25 dB HL), while 167 ([52%]) had normal hearing. Participants with hearing loss were older than those with normal hearing, with a median age of 74 years (IQR 67–79) compared with 60 years (IQR 56–67), respectively. The majority of participants with valid audiometry were assessed at the research site (319/423, 75.4%), with smaller proportions assessed at home (24/423, 5.7%) and in care homes (20/423, 4.7%). Further details are presented in Table 1.


6 Prevalence and severity of hearing loss
Hearing loss was common among participants with valid audiometry, with [46.3%] demonstrating at least mild impairment. The distribution of hearing loss severity is shown in Table 2. Among participants assessed at the research site, 90 (24.9%) had mild hearing impairment, 50 (13.8%) had moderate impairment and 12 (3.3%) had moderately severe impairment. There were marked differences in hearing loss by assessment setting. A higher proportion of participants assessed in care homes had hearing loss compared with those assessed in the community. Severity also varied by setting, with more advanced hearing loss observed among care-home residents. These differences are illustrated in Figure 1.


7 Self-reported hearing difficulty and agreement with audiometric hearing loss
Self-reported hearing difficulty was substantially lower than the prevalence of audiometrically defined hearing loss. Overall, 152 participants (27.1%) reported hearing difficulties. Among participants with audiometrically defined hearing loss, a substantial proportion did not report hearing difficulty, indicating under-recognition of hearing impairment. Conversely, a smaller proportion of participants without measurable hearing loss reported hearing difficulty. These findings demonstrate incomplete agreement between audiometric and self-reported measures of hearing. The relationship between measured hearing loss and self-reported hearing difficulty is summarised in Table 3 and illustrated in Figure 2.


8 Hearing aid use
Hearing aid use data were available for 415 participants. Among those with audiometrically defined hearing loss, [68/196] ([40%]) reported current hearing aid use, while [128/196] ([60%]) did not. Among participants with normal hearing, [6/227] ([2.8%]) reported hearing aid use. The distribution of hearing aid use by hearing status is presented in Table 4. Hearing aid uptake varied by severity of hearing loss, with higher usage observed among participants with more severe impairment
crumbs: 4  Our findings in relation to hearing impairment
